text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions in the left lower quadrant, clearing the left side wall and pelvis. The small bowel is visible beneath the right-sided colostomy, complicating port placement on that side.

The surgeon carefully clears small bowel adhesions from the area surrounding the colostomy to create space for port placement in the right lower quadrant. The adhesions are visibly manageable, allowing for efficient dissection.

The surgeon switches to a left-sided dock and begins a retrorectus dissection, starting just to the right of the midline. The peritoneum and preperitoneal fat are taken down, and the dissection enters the retrorectus space on the left side. The left posterior rectus sheath is separated from the linea alba, extending into the pelvis space of Retzius. The midline is dissected, revealing both rectus muscles, aiding in the development of the space of Retzius. The posterior sheath dissection is advanced towards the subxiphoid area, where the surgeon's left arm is positioned.

The surgeon identifies the semilunar line and carefully preserves a visible neurovascular bundle. The posterior elements in the peritoneum are being preserved as the tissue is eventrated into the hernia defect.

The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side, staying in the pre-transversalis plane to dissect inferiorly and posteriorly towards the hernia defect. A sponge is placed to manage minor bleeding. The dissection progresses towards the superior aspect of the hernia defect, with the right arm positioned below the colostomy in the lower right quadrant.

The surgeon continues dissection, revealing the scarred tissue at the superior aspect of the hernia defect. The dissected plane is visible as the surgeon moves behind the hernia defect, extending laterally during the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.

The surgeon transects the hernia sac, revealing the left epigastric artery on the medial border of the hernia. The dissection continues as the surgeon frees the hernia sac from the peritoneum around the hernia defect, re-entering the pre-transversalis plane laterally. The bowel is carefully maneuvered out of the way to facilitate further lateral dissection.

The surgeon identifies an indirect lipoma ligament and removes fat from the abdominal wall, revealing the preperitoneal space. Using a sponge, the surgeon dissects the myopectineal orifice on the left-hand side, ensuring the mesh will conform to the area for hernia defect coverage. The bladder is carefully dissected free from the pubic bone.

The surgeon achieves a clear view of the left myopectineal orifice and completes a superior to inferior left TAR. The hernia defect measures 13 by 11 cm. The surgeon reduces pressure to 6 and begins closing the defect using a zero V-lock suture, carefully invocating the pseudosac while ensuring the suture does not penetrate the skin. The surgeon starts suturing from the bottom, navigating around the epigastric artery to secure the medial edge of the hernia wall.

The surgeon carefully stitches around the epigastric vessels without ligating them, as they move further from the hernia defect edge. The hernia defect is visibly closed using two long sutures, tied together and run in opposite directions. The surgeon measures the surgical space, noting dimensions of approximately thirty-five centimeters north to south and twenty centimeters east to west.

The surgeon places a biosynthetic mesh over the primary hernia defect, measuring 25 centimeters from north to south and 18 centimeters from east to west. A lightweight mesh is used to cover the myopectineal orifice, ensuring coverage despite the limited size of the biosynthetic mesh.

The surgeon uses 2-0 silk sutures to fixate the mesh in multiple locations: below the colostomy, adjoining the inguinal hernia mesh and synthetic mesh at the inferior border, the lateral border, and the medial side of the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses an absorbable tacking device to secure the flap back to the patient's right side after closing the posterior defect in the peritoneum. The flap is positioned to cover the mesh completely, demonstrating good redundancy.
